skill.name: ai-rag-pipeline
skill.description: Build retrieval augmented generation pipelines that combine web search, content extraction, and LLM analysis through the inference.sh CLI.
skill.summary: Create source-grounded research and answer pipelines with search tools, extractors, and hosted LLMs.
skill.author: inference-sh

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many external command patterns, but most are false positives from markdown fences, inline app identifiers, and documented shell examples. The confirmed concerns are a remote install command piped to sh and intentional external network calls to search, extraction, and LLM services. No prompt injection text, credential theft, persistence, or malicious intent was found in SKILL.md.

medium_findings.title: Remote Install Script Piped To Shell
medium_findings.description: The quick start instructs users to download an installer from https://cli.inference.sh and pipe it directly to sh. This is a supply-chain risk because users execute remote code without local inspection or integrity verification. The context indicates an installation workflow, not confirmed malware.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The pipe-to-shell command is explicit and executable. Confidence is below critical because the surrounding context is normal CLI installation guidance and no malicious payload is present in the skill file.

medium_findings.title: External Search And LLM Service Calls
medium_findings.description: The skill intentionally sends queries, URLs, extracted content, and prompts to inference.sh apps for Tavily, Exa, OpenRouter, Claude, GPT-4o, and Gemini workflows. This is expected for RAG, but users may expose sensitive queries or private source material to third-party services.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples clearly call external search, extraction, and model apps. The risk depends on user input sensitivity, so this is a privacy and data handling warning rather than confirmed abuse.

medium_findings.title: Shell Interpolation In Reusable Research Function
medium_findings.description: The reusable bash function interpolates a user-provided query into a JSON string for an infsh command. This can break JSON formatting or change request semantics if the query contains quotes or shell metacharacters.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The function accepts a positional argument and interpolates it into a quoted JSON payload. The command target is constrained to infsh, but robust JSON construction is not demonstrated.

low_findings.title: Static Backtick Findings Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: Many reported Ruby backtick and template literal findings map to markdown code fences, diagrams, table entries, or inline app identifiers. They do not execute inside the skill file and are not evidence of hidden code execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line review confirms these locations are documentation formatting or sample commands. They are not embedded executable Ruby, JavaScript, or runtime code.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Text Matches
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm alerts appear to match ordinary words inside the description and documentation links, not cryptographic API use. No hashing, encryption, signing, or credential handling code was found in SKILL.md.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are metadata and a documentation link. There is no cryptographic implementation or security-sensitive algorithm selection in this markdown file.

dangerous_patterns.title: Pipe To Shell Installation Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: A remote installer is fetched over HTTPS and executed by sh in a single command. Users should prefer package managers, checksums, signatures, or local inspection before executing installer scripts.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact curl-to-sh pattern is present on the flagged line. This is a known risky installation pattern even when the source may be legitimate.

dangerous_patterns.title: Networked RAG Pipeline With Third-Party Providers
dangerous_patterns.description: The documented pipelines call external search, extraction, and LLM services through inference.sh. Users should avoid sending secrets, private documents, or regulated data unless provider terms and controls are acceptable.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The external app calls are explicit and central to the skill behavior. The security impact depends on the sensitivity of user-supplied inputs.

content.value_statement: Research workflows need current sources, extraction, and grounded analysis without manual copy and paste. This skill shows how to combine web search, source extraction, and LLM prompts into repeatable RAG pipelines.
actual_capabilities: Explains RAG pipeline patterns for search, extraction, augmentation, and generation.
Provides inference.sh CLI examples for Tavily Search, Exa Search, Exa Answer, and OpenRouter models.
Shows multi-source research flows that compare search results before generating an answer.
Demonstrates fact-checking prompts that ask for verdicts, supporting evidence, contradictions, and sources.
Includes a reusable bash function pattern for query-driven research summaries.
limitations: Requires the inference.sh CLI, account access, and configured provider availability.
Network calls may send prompts, queries, URLs, and extracted content to third-party services.
Examples are documentation patterns and do not provide a packaged production application.
Citation quality depends on the search results and the selected LLM response behavior.

use_cases.title: Research briefing
use_cases.description: Build a repeatable workflow that searches multiple sources and produces a sourced topic summary.

use_cases.title: Claim verification
use_cases.description: Check a claim against current web evidence and request a balanced verdict with supporting and contradicting sources.

use_cases.title: Agent knowledge tool
use_cases.description: Add search and answer steps to an AI assistant that needs current context before responding.

prompt_templates.title: Run a basic sourced answer
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner workflow for one search and one answer.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to create a simple RAG flow for: [question]. Search for current sources, summarize the answer, and include citations.

prompt_templates.title: Compare multiple search sources
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Tavily and Exa style searches for: [topic]. Compare recurring themes, disagreements, source quality, and open questions.

prompt_templates.title: Fact-check a public claim
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a RAG fact-checking pipeline for this claim: [claim]. Return a verdict, supporting evidence, contradicting evidence, and source notes.

prompt_templates.title: Design a reusable research agent step
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a reusable research function for: [domain]. Include query construction, source extraction, summarization, citation requirements, and privacy safeguards.

output_examples.input: Research the latest enterprise use of retrieval augmented generation.
output_examples.output: A concise briefing with search themes, notable vendor examples, implementation risks, and source citations.

output_examples.input: Fact-check a claim about AI replacing half of jobs by 2030.
output_examples.output: A verdict with supporting studies, conflicting evidence, confidence level, and recommended wording for publication.

output_examples.input: Summarize two provided industry reports from their URLs.
output_examples.output: A structured comparison covering key findings, data points, disagreements, and recommended follow-up questions.

best_practices: Use specific queries that include topic, timeframe, geography, and desired source type.
Ask the model to separate evidence, analysis, uncertainty, and citations.
Review provider privacy terms before sending confidential prompts, URLs, or extracted text.
anti_patterns: Do not send secrets, customer data, or regulated documents to external services without approval.
Do not treat generated citations as valid until the referenced sources are checked.
Do not use vague searches when a targeted query would produce more reliable evidence.

faq.question: What does this skill help build?
faq.answer: It helps build RAG workflows that search the web, extract context, and ask an LLM to answer with sources.

faq.question: Does it include production application code?
faq.answer: No. It provides command examples, prompt patterns, and pipeline templates for use with inference.sh.

faq.question: Which providers are referenced?
faq.answer: The skill references Tavily, Exa, OpenRouter models, Claude, GPT-4o, and Gemini through inference.sh app commands.

faq.question: Can it be used with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The marketplace metadata marks the skill as compatible with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: What data leaves my environment?
faq.answer: Queries, prompts, URLs, extracted content, and generated context may be sent to external providers through inference.sh.

faq.question: What security warning should users know?
faq.answer: The quick start uses a remote installer piped to sh. Users should inspect installer scripts or use verified installation methods.
